era_context: The Frankish Empire under Louis the Pious is consolidating power after Charlemagne's death in 814. The Abbasid Caliphate under Harun al-Rashid is in its final years, with succession tensions building. Tang China under Emperor Xianzong is experiencing renewed centralization. Byzantine Empire under Nikephoros I faces military and financial pressures.

events.title: Harun al-Rashid's Final Years and Succession Planning
events.description: Caliph Harun al-Rashid (r. 786–809), one of the most celebrated Abbasid rulers, was in declining health in 808. He was making final arrangements for the succession of his sons, designating al-Amin as caliph and al-Ma'mun as heir to the eastern provinces. These arrangements would prove unstable and lead to civil war after his death in 809. Harun's reign had been marked by military campaigns, cultural patronage, and the expansion of Abbasid power.

sources.name: al-Tabari, History of the Prophets and Kings

events.certainty_note: Harun's health decline and succession planning are documented in later Islamic sources, though specific events in 808 are not always precisely dated.

events.title: Byzantine-Abbasid Border Conflicts
events.description: Ongoing military tensions and raids along the Byzantine-Abbasid frontier in Anatolia and northern Syria. Byzantine Emperor Nikephoros I was attempting to strengthen frontier defenses and recover lost territories, while Abbasid forces under Harun al-Rashid's command conducted raids and counter-raids. These conflicts were part of a centuries-long pattern of Byzantine-Islamic warfare.
events.certainty_note: General pattern of conflict is well-established; specific events in 808 are less clearly documented.

events.title: Tang Dynasty Administrative Reforms Continue
events.description: Emperor Xianzong of Tang continued his program of administrative centralization and reduction of regional military governor autonomy. In 808, these reforms were ongoing, with the emperor working to strengthen central control over provincial administrations and military forces. The period saw economic growth and cultural development, though regional tensions persisted.
events.certainty_note: Xianzong's general policies are well-documented; specific events in 808 are not always precisely dated.

disconfirming_evidence: No major disconfirming evidence identified for events listed. However, the specificity of events in 808 is limited by source documentation. Many events attributed to this year in secondary sources are actually approximate datings within a broader period.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 808 is moderate, with sources strongest for the Abbasid Caliphate and Tang China. Byzantine sources exist but are often retrospective. Frankish sources are less detailed for this specific year. Sub-Saharan Africa, the Americas, and much of Southeast Asia have no written records. The surviving sources reflect elite, court-centered perspectives.

graph_edges.note: Harun's succession planning led directly to civil war after his death in 809
